nativeRange.habitat: Turbid, nutrient-rich reef environments; lagoons, reef slopes, and protected reef zones with lower light and gentle flow

decorPreferences: stable base with no toppling risk

diet.notes: Primarily photosynthetic via symbiotic zooxanthellae. Has been reported to benefit from weekly target feeding of micro-plankton, mysis shrimp, or brine shrimp; polyps extend noticeably during feeding events. No tentacles are present; feeding occurs via the fleshy tissue lobes.

goodWith: other peaceful LPS corals with adequate spacing
avoidWith: aggressive LPS corals with long sweeper tentacles
species that may nip at tissue lobes

summary: Nemenzophyllia turbida (Fox Coral) is a peaceful LPS coral with distinctive ruffled tissue lobes and no tentacles. Generally advised for intermediate reefers; prefers low flow, moderate light, and lower tank placement.
careNotes:
Nemenzophyllia turbida is the sole species in its genus and is commonly known in the aquarium trade as Fox Coral, Jasmine Coral, or Ridge Coral. It is classified as a Large Polyp Stony (LPS) coral in family Caryophylliidae (order Scleractinia). Note: some recent sources (including Reeflings) place it in family Euphyllidae under current taxonomy revisions; Caryophylliidae is retained here as the traditionally cited family in the aquarium hobby.

Key care requirements:
• Lighting: Generally advised to keep under moderate light (approximately 75–150 PAR). Fox Coral naturally inhabits turbid, lower-light reef environments and tends to do poorly under high-intensity lighting. Acclimate gradually if moving from a dim setting.
• Flow: Low flow is strongly recommended. This species lacks tentacles and has very delicate ruffled tissue lobes that can be damaged or shredded by strong direct flow. Gentle, indirect water movement that avoids buffeting the polyps is ideal.
• Placement: Lower areas of the aquarium — bottom rockwork or sandbed — are generally preferred. The calcareous skeleton is notably fragile; secure placement to prevent toppling, which can crack the skeleton and expose tissue to injury or infection.
• Feeding: Has been reported to benefit from weekly target feeding of meaty foods (mysis shrimp, micro-plankton, brine shrimp). Feeding response is visible as tissue lobe extension. No sweeper tentacles are present — feeding occurs via direct contact with the expanded tissue lobes.
• Aggression: One of the more peaceful LPS corals; lacks tentacles and produces no sweeper extensions. However, adequate spacing (several centimeters) from neighboring corals is still advisable, as it can be stung by aggressive neighbors and cannot defend itself.
• Skeleton fragility: The skeleton is unusually fragile compared to most LPS corals. Handle with care when transporting or repositioning; even minor drops or impacts can fracture the skeleton. This is a key intermediate-care consideration despite otherwise straightforward husbandry.
• Taxonomy note: Also recorded under the junior synonym Pleurogyra turbida in some older trade sources (including a LiveAquaria listing variant); Nemenzophyllia turbida is the accepted name per WoRMS and current literature.
• Water chemistry: Requires standard reef supplementation of calcium (420–440 ppm), magnesium (1260–1350 ppm), and alkalinity (8–12 dKH). Strontium and trace elements are also cited by multiple sources as beneficial.

Cross-source notes: LiveAquaria and AquariumDomain both list care level as 'Easy', but the skeletal fragility, target feeding requirement, and low-flow sensitivity elevate practical difficulty to intermediate for typical hobbyists — consistent with the task specification. Temperature range averaged across sources: LiveAquaria/AquariumDomain 72–78°F vs. Reeflings 75–82°F → 23–27°C adopted. PAR range from Reeflings (100–150) and task specification (75–150) → 75–150 adopted.
breedingNotes: Fragging is possible but uncommon compared to other LPS corals, as the fragile skeleton makes clean cuts difficult. Sexual reproduction occurs via broadcast spawning on natural reefs but is not practiced in the aquarium hobby.

additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (24–28°C vs. LiveAquaria 22–25.6°C), salinity (1.023–1.026), pH (8.0–8.4), PAR (100–150), flow (moderate with delicate polyp caution), placement (middle). Temperature upper bound 28°C from Reeflings averaged with LiveAquaria 25.6°C → 27°C max adopted. Also lists family as Euphyllidae rather than Caryophylliidae — noted in careNotes.

additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (72–78°F = 22–25.6°C), pH (8.1–8.4), salinity (1.023–1.025), dKH (8–12), flow (low), placement (bottom), temperament (peaceful). Parameters consistent with LiveAquaria; confirms dKH 8–12 and bottom placement.
